question: Are there Consequential Damages on the Project?
raw_answer: The Contractor and Owner waive Claims against each other for consequential, special, indirect, exemplary, or punitive damages arising out of or relating to this Contract.

question: What are the are the Consequential Damages Terms & Conditions?
value: The Contractor and Owner waive Claims against each other for consequential, special, indirect, exemplary, or punitive damages arising out of or relating to this Contract. This mutual waiver includes, without limitation: damages incurred by the Owner for additional interest, penalties, taxes, insurance, additional marketing or management costs, rental expenses, for losses of use, income, profit, financing, business and reputation to Owner or to the Project, and for loss of management or employee productivity or of the services of such persons and for lost or deferred sales, rents or use of property including the Project or portions thereof or reduced sales or rent prices or diminution in value, and damages incurred by the Contractor for principal office expenses including the compensation of personnel stationed there, for losses of financing, business and reputation, and for loss of profit except anticipated profit arising directly from the Work. This mutual waiver is applicable, without limitation, to all consequential damages due to either party's termination of the Contract as provided in the Contract Documents. Nothing contained in this Section 4.3.10 shall be deemed to preclude an award of agreed liquidated damages in accordance with the requirements of the Agreement.
raw_answer: The Contractor and Owner waive Claims against each other for consequential, special, indirect, exemplary, or punitive damages arising out of or relating to this Contract.

question: Is there a mutual waiver of Consequential Damages in the Contract?
raw_answer: The Contractor and Owner waive Claims against each other for consequential, special, indirect, exemplary, or punitive damages arising out of or relating to this Contract.

question: Are there Liquidated Damages on the Project?
raw_answer: The Contractor shall pay the Owner liquidated damages, as fair compensation (and not as a penalty), in accordance with the following: $2,000 per day for each day beyond the Substantial Completion date.

question: What are the are the Liquidated Damages Terms & Conditions?
value: The Owner will suffer substantial hardship and incur substantial damages if the Work is not completed on time, or if the Contractor fails to achieve Substantial Completion on or before the scheduled Substantial Completion date set forth above, as such date may be modified under the Contract Documents. The parties agree that such damages are difficult or impossible to determine with certainty, and therefore have in good faith estimated as fair compensation (and not as a penalty) the liquidated damages set forth below. If the Contractor fails to achieve Substantial Completion on or before the scheduled date set forth above, as such date may be modified in accordance with the Contract Documents, the Contractor shall pay the Owner liquidated damages, as fair compensation (and not as a penalty), in accordance with the following: $2,000 per day for each day beyond the Substantial Completion date. The assessment of liquidated damages shall be paid per calendar day until Substantial Completion shall be achieved. Liquidated damages shall be assessed cumulatively in accordance with the Contract Documents. Contractor hereby expressly agrees to waive its right to challenge the enforceability of this provision, and agrees that the liquidated damages amounts represent a reasonable measurement of damages, and not as a penalty, that Owner will sustain in the event of late performance. These liquidated damages are in lieu of Owner’s actual direct or consequential damages or losses as a result of Contractor's failure to achieve Substantial Completion of the Work. The Owner may deduct liquidated damages from any amounts due or to become due Contractor, or the Owner may require Contractor to pay any liquidated damages which exceed amounts due or to become due to Contractor within ten (10) days after Owner’s written request. Contractor waives and Owner is not required to comply with any other notice provisions in order to assess and/or deduct liquidated damages. Owner may assess and/or deduct the amount of liquidated damages by withholding the amount of the liquidated damages from the payment due to Contractor on any pay application(s) and paying that amount to itself.
raw_answer: $2,000 per day for each day beyond the Substantial Completion date

question: Is a Payment & Performance Bond Required for this project?
raw_answer: The Owner shall have the right to require the Contractor to furnish bonds, subject to Owner’s approval, which cover Contractor’s faithful performance of the Contract and payment of obligations arising thereunder.

question: What is the Payment & Performance Bond Article #?
What are the Payment & Performance Bond Article Terms & Conditions?
value: The Payment & Performance Bond Article Terms & Conditions are as follows: The Owner has the right to require the Contractor to furnish bonds that cover the Contractor’s faithful performance of the Contract and payment of obligations arising thereunder. Upon request of the Owner, the Contractor must purchase and maintain a Performance and Labor and Materials Bond executed by a Surety with a minimum rating of A.M. Best A, in favor of the Owner, on a standard AIA bond form, A312. This bond must be in an amount equal to 100% of the Contract Price and must remain in full force and effect through the warranty and extended warranty periods. The Contractor must promptly furnish a copy of the bonds to any potential beneficiary upon request. Any defaults or claims in excess of the limits of bonds provided or SDI purchased must be paid by the General Contractor without an increase to the GMP. The Contractor must pay all costs arising from a Subcontractor’s default if the limits are exhausted and must indemnify the Owner from all claims arising from such defaults.

question: What is the IMC Design Document Review obligations Article #?
What are the Terms & Conditions / IMC's obligations for Design Document Review?
value: The Contractor is obligated to carefully study and compare the various Drawings and other Contract Documents before starting the Work and each portion of the Work. The Contractor must take field measurements of any existing conditions related to that portion of the Work and observe any conditions at the site affecting it. The Contractor shall immediately report to the Owner and the Architect, in writing, any error, inconsistency, omission, or problem with any construction feasibility it discovers. Any necessary change shall be ordered in accordance with Article 7, subject to the requirements of the provisions of the Contract Documents. If the Contractor proceeds with work without such notice to the Architect and the Owner, having discovered such errors, inconsistencies, or problems with construction feasibility, or if by such reasonable study of the site and the Contract Documents he should have discovered such, the Contractor shall bear all costs that would have been avoided by not performing the obligations in this article.

question: What is the Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, & Contract Document Deviations Article #?
What are the Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, & Contract Document Deviations Terms & Conditions?
value: The terms and conditions for Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, and Contract Document Deviations are as follows: The Contractor must inform the Architect and the Owner in writing of any proposed material deviations from the Contract Documents at the time of submission for approval. The Contractor must provide evidence that the proposed substitution or deviation will deliver a quality result equal to the original specifications. If the Architect or Owner finds the evidence insufficient, they may reject the substitution or deviation. If approved, the Contractor is responsible for any additional work and materials required due to the deviation, at no cost to the Owner and without project delays, unless the Owner or Architect is the sole cause. Approval of a substitution does not relieve the Contractor from liability for non-compliance with the Contract Documents. The Architect is responsible for reviewing and incorporating Owner-requested substitutions into the design. The Contractor is also responsible for ensuring that all work complies with approved submittals and for informing the Architect in writing of any deviations at the time of submittal. The Contractor is not relieved of responsibility for errors or omissions in submittals by the Architect's approval.
raw_answer: Contractor must inform Architect and Owner in writing of deviations, provide evidence of quality, and is responsible for additional work and materials if approved.

question: What is the Precon Reimbursable Contract Article #?
What are the Precon Reimbursable Terms & Conditions?
value: The Precon Reimbursable Terms & Conditions include the following: The General Conditions Amount is the amount paid to reimburse the Contractor for all General Conditions costs, not exceeding $1,470,077.00. Labor costs include wages of construction workers, supervisory and administrative personnel, taxes, insurance, contributions, assessments, and benefits required by law or collective bargaining agreements. The Cost of the Work shall be credited with all rebates, trade discounts, and credits. Subcontract costs, royalties, license fees, data processing costs, legal, mediation, and arbitration costs, and other costs incurred in the performance of the Work are also reimbursable. Costs due to emergencies and costs of repairing or correcting damaged or nonconforming Work are included. The Contractor is not entitled to reimbursement for expenses of the principal office, overhead and general expenses, capital expenses, rental costs of machinery and equipment, costs due to negligence, and any cost not specifically described in Article 5. Discounts, rebates, and refunds obtained by the Contractor shall accrue to the Owner. The Contractor shall only be entitled to reimbursement for direct expenditures and reasonable costs incurred as a result of termination, and for Work actually performed.

question: What is the Subcontractor Required Number of Bids per package?
What are the Subcontractor Bids / Bid Tabs Provided to Owner Terms & Conditions?
value: The terms and conditions for Subcontractor Bids/Bid Tabs provided to the Owner include the following: The Contractor must deliver bids from Subcontractors and suppliers to the Architect, and the Owner will determine which bid to accept with the advice of the Contractor. If the Owner requires a different bid to be accepted than the one recommended by the Contractor, a Change Order may be issued to adjust the Guaranteed Maximum Price. Subcontracts must conform to the payment provisions of the Agreement and Contract Documents and cannot be awarded on a cost-plus-fee basis without the Owner's prior written consent. The Contractor must provide the Owner with copies of all subcontracts within thirty days of execution. The Owner may designate specific persons or entities from whom the Contractor must obtain bids. The Contractor must furnish the names of proposed persons or entities for each principal portion of the Work to the Owner through the Architect within 120 days after the award of the Contract. The Architect and Owner have the right to object to any proposed person or entity. Subcontract agreements must bind Subcontractors to the terms of the Contract Documents and preserve the rights of the Owner and Architect. The Contractor must also provide the Owner with releases of claims and other documents as requested to evidence payment and discharge of liens.

question: What is the GMP Shared Savings Article #?
What are the GMP Shared Savings Terms & Conditions?
value: To the extent that the Cost of the Work and the Contractor’s Fee is less than the Guaranteed Maximum Price, the Contractor shall be entitled to share such “savings” realized by the Owner in connection with the Project as follows: --first $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 30% to the Contractor and 70% to Owner; --second $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 25% to the Contractor and 75% to Owner; --third $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 20% to the Contractor and 80% to Owner; --fourth $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 15% to the Contractor and 85% to Owner; --fifth $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 10% to the Contractor and 90% to Owner. All remaining savings shall be to and for the benefit of the Owner. The Contractor’s share of the savings as broken down above shall be capped at $100,000.
raw_answer: Contractor shares savings with Owner based on a tiered distribution, capped at $100,000 for the Contractor.

question: What is the GMP Buyout Savings Article #?
Do GMP Buyout Savings need to be Reported to Owner?
raw_answer: Yes, GMP Buyout Savings need to be reported to Owner.

question: Must the GMP Buyout Savings be Shown on Monthly Schedule of Values?
raw_answer: Yes, the GMP Buyout Savings must be shown on the Monthly Schedule of Values.

question: What are the GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions?
value: The GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions are as follows: The Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP) is the total maximum cost to be paid by the Owner for the Contractor’s full and complete performance under the Contract Documents, including all services, labor, materials, equipment, and fees. If the Cost of the Work and the Contractor’s Fee is less than the GMP, the Contractor is entitled to share the savings realized by the Owner. The distribution of savings is tiered: the first $100,000 of savings is distributed 30% to the Contractor and 70% to the Owner; the second $100,000 is distributed 25% to the Contractor and 75% to the Owner; the third $100,000 is distributed 20% to the Contractor and 80% to the Owner; the fourth $100,000 is distributed 15% to the Contractor and 85% to the Owner; and the fifth $100,000 is distributed 10% to the Contractor and 90% to the Owner. All remaining savings benefit the Owner. The Contractor’s share of the savings is capped at $100,000.
raw_answer: The GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions outline the distribution of savings if the Cost of the Work and the Contractor’s Fee is less than the GMP, with a tiered distribution and a cap on the Contractor’s share.

question: Must Use of Buyout Savings for Trade Costs be Approved by Owner?
raw_answer: Yes, use of buyout savings for trade costs must be approved by the Owner.

question: What is the Contingency Usage Article #?
value: Article 6
raw_answer: Article 6

question: What are the Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions?
value: The Contractor must submit a Contingency Use Authorization form for the Owner's review and approval before using any part of the Contractor's Contingency. The form must detail the amount and basis for the proposed use and include supporting documentation. The Contractor cannot use the Contingency for General Conditions costs or any amount exceeding the threshold without prior approval from the Owner. Unauthorized use of the Contingency must be returned to the Owner immediately. Monthly reports on the use of the Contingency are required, and the Owner has the discretion to reject any proposed or actual use that does not meet the Contract requirements. The Contingency can be used for costs arising from design refinements, corrective work not due to the Contractor's fault, nonperformance by Subcontractors, unanticipated costs during construction, and unintended gaps or overruns in the buy-out of the Work. It cannot be used for work due to the Contractor's negligence, the Contractor's Fee, costs recoverable from Subcontractors or insurance, or any item not constituting a Cost of the Work. Requests to use the Contingency for amounts over $35,000 or to increase the General Conditions line item require a Contingency Use Authorization form.
raw_answer: The Contractor must submit a Contingency Use Authorization form for the Owner's review and approval before using any part of the Contractor's Contingency. Unauthorized use must be returned immediately. Monthly reports are required, and the Owner can reject any use that does not meet Contract requirements. The Contingency can be used for specific costs but not for the Contractor's negligence, Fee, or recoverable costs.

question: What are the Contingency Usage Owner Notification Requirements?
value: Contractor must submit a Contingency Use Authorization form for Owner’s review and approval, fully describing the amount and basis for the proposed use and providing documentation support. The Contractor cannot use any part of the Contingency for General Conditions costs without prior submission and Owner’s written approval. Any use of the Contingency without prior submission and approval is invalid and must be returned to the Owner. Additionally, the Contractor must provide a monthly written report describing the use of the Contingency, which will be reconciled with the Owner during the pay application review process. The Owner has discretion to reject any proposed or actual use of the Contingency that does not meet the Contract requirements.

The Owner and the Owner's accountants shall be afforded reasonable access to, and shall be permitted to audit and copy upon reasonable advance notice, the Contractor's records, books, correspondence, instructions, drawings, receipts, subcontracts, purchase orders, vouchers, memoranda and other data relating to this Contract, and the Contractor shall preserve these for a period of three (3) years after final payment, or for such longer period as may be required by law. What is the Adverse Weather Days Article #?
What are the Adverse Weather Days Terms & Conditions?
value: If adverse weather conditions are the basis for a Claim for additional time, such Claim shall be documented by data substantiating the weather conditions were abnormal for the period of time as compared with the ten-year average for the local geographical area where the Project is situated, could not have been reasonably anticipated and had an adverse effect on the critical path schedule. The Contractor assumes the risks of the normal weather patterns of the local area in which the Project is located. No claim for a time extension shall be allowed on account of a failure of the Architect to furnish interpretations, instructions or drawings or to return Shop Drawings or samples until ten (10) workdays after receipt by the Architect of a written request for such instructions, drawings, or samples and only if such delay impacts the Project’s critical path and Contractor has complied with the notice and proof provisions set forth in Article 4. Contractor shall not be allowed an extension of time unless Contractor has established to the Owner’s satisfaction that the delay claimed by the Contractor is to a portion of the Work on the critical path of the Contract Schedule, and the Project is currently behind the current Contract Schedule.
raw_answer: Adverse weather conditions must be documented as abnormal compared to the ten-year average and must have an adverse effect on the critical path schedule. The Contractor assumes the risks of normal weather patterns. No time extension claims are allowed for delays in receiving instructions or drawings unless it impacts the critical path and the Contractor has complied with notice and proof provisions. Extensions are only allowed if the delay affects the critical path and the project is behind schedule.

question: Within how many days must we notify the owner of adverse weather day impacts?
raw_answer: ten (10) workdays

question: How many Adverse Weather Days Included in Contract Agreement?
What is the Schedule Requirements Article #?
What are the Schedule Requirements Terms & Conditions?
value: The Contract Schedule shall be based on an orderly progression of the Work, allowing adequate time for each operation, and leading to Substantial Completion by the date(s) established in the Agreement. The Contractor must coordinate its work with the work of other third-party contractors. The Contract Schedule will be reviewed by the Owner and the Architect for compliance with the requirements of this Article and will be accepted by them only in the form of an approved Change Order. Schedules submitted or used by the Contractor that are not the current Contract Schedule accepted in an approved Change Order are for the sole use of the Contractor, at his risk, and do not relate to this Contract. Unless specifically required by law, no initial payment under this Contract shall be due until the Contract Schedule has been approved by the Owner and the Architect. If the Contractor and Owner have agreed that the Contractor should be permitted to extend the time for completion as provided in Subparagraph 4.3.7, the calendar dates in the Contract Schedule shall be adjusted accordingly to retain their same relationship to the adjusted date of Substantial Completion, and the dollar value of work to be completed as the first of each month shall be adjusted pro-rata. The Contractor shall furnish sufficient forces, plant and equipment and shall work such hours, including night shifts and lawful overtime operations as may be necessary to ensure the prosecution of the work in accordance with the Contract Schedule. If, in the opinion of the Owner, the contractor falls behind schedule, the Contractor must take such steps as may be necessary to improve his progress, and must submit his plans demonstrating the manner in which the desired rate of progress may be regained. It shall be the responsibility of the Contractor to maintain his schedule so as not to delay the progress of the Work or the scheduled work of other persons. If Contractor delays the progress of the Work, in order to regain the time lost and to maintain the Contract Schedule, it shall be the responsibility of the Contractor to increase the number of shifts, days of Work, and/or to the extent permitted by law, to institute or increase overtime operations, all without additional cost to the Owner in excess of the GMP.
raw_answer: The Contract Schedule shall be based on an orderly progression of the Work, allowing adequate time for each operation, and leading to Substantial Completion by the date(s) established in the Agreement.

question: What is the Schedule Delay / Time Issues Article #?
What are the Schedule Delay / Time Issues Terms & Conditions?
value: The Schedule Delay / Time Issues Terms & Conditions are detailed in various sections of the contract. If the Owner issues a written order to proceed and approves the Contractor's claim, the Contract Sum and/or Contract Time will be adjusted by Change Order. The Contractor must proceed with the work without delay, even if the claim is approved or denied in part or in whole. Claims for additional time must be made in writing and include an estimate of the probable effect of delay on the progress of the Work. The Contractor is entitled to an extension of time for delays caused by the Owner, Architect/Engineer, other contractors, changes in the Work, or certain uncontrollable events like natural disasters or government restrictions. However, the Contractor waives any claim for damages due to delays and can only claim an extension of time. Adverse weather conditions must be documented and proven to be abnormal and impactful on the critical path schedule. No time extension claims are allowed for delays caused by the Architect unless specific conditions are met. The Contractor must prove that the delay affects the critical path and that the Project is behind schedule. The Contractor must also maintain the schedule and take necessary steps to improve progress if they fall behind, without additional cost to the Owner.
raw_answer: The Schedule Delay / Time Issues Terms & Conditions are detailed in various sections of the contract, including provisions for claims, extensions of time, and conditions under which delays are excusable.

question: How much Schedule Delay / Time Issues Notice to Owner Requirements (Within What # of Days)?
raw_answer: ten (10) workdays

question: What is the Schedule Acceleration Directed by Owner Article #?
What are the Schedule Acceleration Directed by Owner Terms & Conditions?
value: In order to expedite the completion of the Work, the Owner, in his sole discretion, may direct the Contractor to increase the number of shifts, days of work, and/or perform overtime operations or to take such other measures as he deems necessary to expedite construction. Such instruction shall be in writing and signed by the Owner. The Contractor, however, shall not be entitled to any additional costs unless the need for such direction was not attributable to the actions, omissions, delays or other fault of the Contractor. In such an event, the Owner shall pay the Contractor the additional cost as mutually agreed upon in writing. If the Contractor is behind schedule due to his own actions, omissions, delays or other fault on its part and the Owner directs the Contractor to work additional shifts, overtime, expedite deliveries, supplement its forces or purchase additional materials or equipment in order to bring his work up to schedule, all additional cost in excess of the GMP shall be borne by the Contractor.
raw_answer: Owner may direct Contractor to expedite work at no additional cost if delays are Contractor's fault.

question: What are the Schedule Definitions of Days (Work / Calendar / Holidays / Etc.)?
value: The term 'day' as used in the Contract Documents shall mean calendar day unless otherwise specifically defined. The term 'working day' shall mean any calendar day except Saturdays, Sundays and Legal Holidays at the place of the Project.
raw_answer: The term 'day' as used in the Contract Documents shall mean calendar day unless otherwise specifically defined. The term 'working day' shall mean any calendar day except Saturdays, Sundays and Legal Holidays at the place of the Project.

question: What is the Substantial Completion Article #?
raw_answer: ARTICLE 3.3

question: What are the Substantial Completion Terms & Conditions?
value: Substantial Completion is the stage when all of the Work is sufficiently complete in accordance with the Contract Documents and all Work under this Contract required to license the building as indicated in the Contract Documents, is complete to allow for a licensing inspection by a licensing agency for that particular location so that the Owner can occupy and utilize the Work for its intended use. However, in no event will Substantial Completion be reached within the meaning of this Contract until: (1) all Work under this Contract is completed to allow for necessary licenses, (2) Certificates of Occupancy are issued by the appropriate governmental authorities provided such certificates are not withheld through no fault of Contractor, (3) all finishes, including but not limited to, wall coverings, paint, carpeting, flooring, appliances, lighting fixtures, plumbing fixtures, softscape, hardscape, and paving have been properly installed (exclusive of Punch List work), (4) all as-built drawings, Operations and Maintenance manuals and warranties have been provided to the Owner, and (5) the Architect has issued a Certificate of Substantial Completion. Issuance of a license is not a criteria for Substantial Completion.

However, in no event will Substantial Completion be reached within the meaning of this Contract until: (1) all Work under this Contract is completed to allow for necessary licenses, (2) Certificates of Occupancy are issued by the appropriate governmental authorities provided such certificates are not withheld through no fault of Contractor, (3) all finishes, including but not limited to, wall coverings, paint, carpeting, flooring, appliances, lighting fixtures, plumbing fixtures, softscape, hardscape, and paving have been properly installed (exclusive of Punch List work), (4) all as-built drawings, Operations and Maintenance manuals and warranties have been provided to the Owner, and (5) the Architect has issued a Certificate of Substantial Completion. Issuance of a license is not a criteria for Substantial Completion.

question: What is the Payment Terms (Progress & Final) Article #?

question: What are the Payment Terms Retainage %?
What is the Payment Terms for Retainage Reduction Article #?
What are the Payment Terms & Conditions for Retainage Reduction?
value: The Owner shall withhold as retainage 10% of each approved progress payment through 50% completion, on a line item basis, of the Project. Once a line item reaches 50% completion no further retainage will be held on that line item. This retainage shall be withheld until Substantial Completion as defined in the Contract Documents. Upon Substantial Completion of the Work by the Contractor, the Contractor shall be paid a sum sufficient to increase the total payments to the Contractor to 100% of the Cost of the Work and Fees, but less an amount equal to 200% of the costs of the Work for incomplete Work or Punch List Work. However, in no event will the retainage be reduced lower than the amount required by the Lender in the Construction Loan Agreement. In the event of any inconsistency between this retainage clause and the Construction Loan Agreement, the Construction Loan Agreement shall govern.

Once a line item reaches 50% completion no further retainage will be held on that line item. This retainage shall be withheld until Substantial Completion as defined in the Contract Documents. Upon Substantial Completion of the Work by the Contractor, the Contractor shall be paid a sum sufficient to increase the total payments to the Contractor to 100% of the Cost of the Work and Fees, but less an amount equal to 200% of the costs of the Work for incomplete Work or Punch List Work. However, in no event will the retainage be reduced lower than the amount required by the Lender in the Construction Loan Agreement. In the event of any inconsistency between this retainage clause and the Construction Loan Agreement, the Construction Loan Agreement shall govern.

question: What is the Payment Terms for Offsite Stored Material Article #?
raw_answer: Article 7.3.8

question: What are the Terms and Conditions for Payment of Offsite Stored Material?
value: Payments for materials and equipment stored offsite may be made if approved in advance by the Owner and stored at a location agreed upon in writing. The Contractor must comply with procedures satisfactory to the Owner to establish the Owner's title to such materials and equipment or otherwise protect the Owner's interest. This includes the costs of applicable insurance, storage, and transportation to the site. The Contractor is responsible for any loss or damage to unincorporated materials or equipment stored offsite not covered by insurance.

The Contractor must comply with procedures satisfactory to the Owner to establish the Owner's title to such materials and equipment or otherwise protect the Owner's interest. This includes the costs of applicable insurance, storage, and transportation to the site. The Contractor is responsible for any loss or damage to unincorporated materials or equipment stored offsite not covered by insurance.

question: How many days within Approved Application for Owner Payment?
raw_answer: 30 days

question: What is the Article # for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?
raw_answer: Section 9.7.1

question: What are the Terms & Conditions for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?
value: If an undisputed amount is due to the Contractor and not paid by the Owner within forty-five (45) days after receipt of the Contractor's Application for Payment, or if the Owner does not pay the Contractor within seven (7) days after the date established in the Contract Documents the amount certified by the Architect, the Contractor may, upon seven (7) additional days' written notice to the Owner and Architect, stop the Work until payment of the amount owing has been received. However, the Contractor shall not stop performance in the event there is a bona fide dispute with the Owner regarding any of the reasons set forth. The performance of the Work shall proceed without delay, but subject to Contractor’s right to assert a claim.
raw_answer: Contractor may stop work if payment is not received within specified timeframes, but not if there is a bona fide dispute.

question: What is the Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Article #?
What are the Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Terms & Conditions?
value: If reasonable precautions will be inadequate to prevent foreseeable bodily injury or death to persons resulting from a material or substance, including but not limited to asbestos or polychlorinated biphenyl (PCB), encountered on the site by the Contractor, the Contractor shall, upon recognizing the condition, immediately stop Work in the affected area and report the condition to the Owner and Architect in writing. The Contractor shall have no responsibility for the discovery, presence, handling, removal, disposal or exposure of persons to hazardous substances existing in any form at the site unless the Contractor or anyone for whom Contractor is liable introduces the hazardous substance to the site, or unless the Contract Documents require the abatement and removal of said hazardous substances. The Owner shall obtain the services of a licensed laboratory to verify the presence or absence of the material or substance reported by the Contractor and, in the event such material or substance is found to be present, to verify that it has been rendered harmless. Unless otherwise required by the Contract Documents, the Owner shall furnish in writing to the Contractor and Architect the names and qualifications of persons or entities who are to perform tests verifying the presence or absence of such material or substance or who are to perform the task of removal or safe containment of such material or substance. The Contractor and the Architect will promptly reply to the Owner in writing. To the fullest extent permitted by law, the Owner shall indemnify and hold harmless the Contractor, and its Subcontractors and employees from and against claims, damages, losses and expenses, including but not limited to attorneys' fees, arising out of or resulting from performance of the Work in the affected area if in fact the material or substance presents the risk of bodily injury or death and has not been rendered harmless, provided that such claim, damage, loss or expense is attributable to bodily injury, sickness, disease or death, or to injury to or destruction of tangible property (other than the Work itself) and provided that such damage, loss or expense is not due to the sole negligence of a party seeking indemnity. The Owner shall not be responsible for materials and substances brought to the site by the Contractor unless such materials or substances were required by the Contract Documents and were obtained, handled and delivered in accordance with the Contract Documents.
raw_answer: Contractor must stop work and report hazardous conditions; Owner handles verification and indemnifies Contractor.

question: List the Owner / Project Required Reports
value: affidavit that required payrolls, bills for materials and equipment, and other indebtedness connected with the Work have been paid or otherwise satisfied
certificate evidencing that insurance required by the Contract Documents to remain in force after final payment is currently in effect
written statement that the Contractor knows of no substantial reason that the insurance will not be renewable to cover the period required by the Contract Documents
consent of surety, if any, to final payment
lien waiver and release forms required under the Contract Documents, executed by Contractor and any other necessary parties
letter copied to the Owner, stating that all close out Operations and Maintenance manuals and warranties have been submitted per Contract Document requirements
correct lists of the Major Subcontractors and all other Subcontractors, suppliers and materialmen employed or retained in connection with the construction of the Improvements
